refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP3, DP6) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular hot-swappable design for rapid component replacement, (3) predictive condition monitoring to detect degradation early, and (4) comprehensive fault detection and isolation with automated diagnostics—all coordinated to achieve target availability ≥0.97 by minimizing both failure frequency and mean-time-to-repair.

potential_sources: Redundancy strategy (DP1 component) must be decoupled from failover logic to avoid tight coupling between detection and switching mechanisms.
Predictive maintenance (DP3 component) depends on sensor data quality and model accuracy; coupling risk if monitoring system shares critical resources with primary control path.
Graceful degradation (DP4 component) introduces coupling if mode-switching logic is distributed across multiple subsystems rather than centralized.
Synchronization in distributed control (DP5 component) can create hidden dependencies if consensus protocols are not carefully isolated from functional logic.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is composite and requires careful decomposition to avoid creating a monolithic 'availability subsystem' that couples all four mechanisms. Each sub-DP should have clear ownership boundaries (IM layer) and well-defined interfaces. Risk: treating availability as a cross-cutting concern rather than a structured set of independent mechanisms.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target availability (≥0.97) is achievable within cost, weight, power, and schedule constraints; no validation of feasibility provided.

ASSUMPTION: 'Agreed evaluation period' is sufficiently long and representative to yield statistically valid availability measurements; short or atypical periods may not reflect true operational availability.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose constraints (e.g., mandatory shutdown windows, restricted maintenance access) that conflict with availability targets.
RISK: Redundancy and modularity increase system complexity, which can paradoxically reduce availability if integration testing and failure mode analysis are inadequate.
RISK: Predictive maintenance models require historical failure data; new systems or novel operating conditions may lack sufficient training data, reducing model reliability.
RISK: Automated failover and diagnostics introduce new failure modes (e.g., false positives, cascading switchovers) that must be explicitly analyzed and tested.
RISK: Distributed control and synchronization mechanisms introduce latency and potential race conditions; availability gains from redundancy may be offset by coordination overhead.
RISK: Measurement methodology for availability is not specified; ambiguity in how downtime is counted (e.g., planned vs. unplanned, partial vs. total loss) can lead to disputes.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'operational availability' (e.g., percentage of time system is capable of performing primary mission function, excluding planned maintenance windows), (2) Acceptable downtime budget per evaluation period (e.g., max 262 hours/year for 0.97 availability), (3) Distinction between full availability and degraded-mode operation (if graceful degradation is acceptable), (4) Constraints on redundancy (cost, weight, power budget), and (5) Measurement methodology and data collection plan. Decompose DP7 into independent sub-DPs with explicit IM ownership: DP7a (Redundancy & Failover), DP7b (Modularity & Replacement), DP7c (Condition Monitoring), DP7d (Fault Detection & Isolation), each with defined interfaces and failure modes. Establish acceptance test plan that validates availability under representative fault injection scenarios, not just nominal operation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability architecture encompassing redundancy coordination, hot-swap capability management, predictive condition monitoring orchestration, and fault detection/isolation/diagnostics across all vehicle subsystems and teleoperation interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests real-time sensor data, subsystem health metrics, and performance telemetry from Vehicle to feed predictive condition monitoring and fault detection algorithms.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends automated failover commands, component replacement directives, and diagnostic instructions to Vehicle subsystems and Teleoperation to execute redundancy switching and hot-swap procedures.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Broadcasts critical fault isolation results, availability status, and automated diagnostic findings to Safety Services for real-time safety decision-making.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Maintains communication with remote monitoring and control systems for predictive analytics updates, failover coordination, and diagnostic data transmission.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestration layer responsible for integrated availability strategy. Redundancy coordination, predictive monitoring, fault detection/isolation, and automated diagnostics are all cross-cutting concerns that naturally belong at the solution architecture level. Minor diffusion: actual failover execution and component replacement mechanics reside in Vehicle; telemetry collection is Vehicle-native; Safety Services consumes alerts but does not own availability logic.

Poor containment. Vehicle owns the physical subsystems and their redundancy hardware, but the integrated availability architecture—especially predictive monitoring, fault isolation algorithms, and automated diagnostics—is a cross-vehicle concern that must coordinate across multiple subsystems and external teleoperation. Placing DP7 here would fragment the availability strategy and couple it tightly to vehicle-specific implementation, reducing reusability and system-level visibility.

Very poor containment. Teleoperation is a user interface and remote control layer. It does not own subsystem redundancy, predictive monitoring, or fault detection logic. Placing DP7 here would scatter availability concerns across the system and create inappropriate coupling between operator control and autonomous availability management.

Poor containment. Safety Services consumes availability and fault information (via SafetyAlert) to make safety decisions, but does not own the availability architecture itself. Placing DP7 here would conflate safety decision-making with availability orchestration, violating separation of concerns and creating circular dependencies.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP7 is a system-level, cross-cutting availability strategy that must coordinate redundancy, monitoring, diagnostics, and failover across all vehicle subsystems and teleoperation interfaces. NGHE Solution, as the integrated solution architecture layer, is the natural owner. It receives telemetry from Vehicle, issues tasking commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation, and publishes safety alerts to Safety Services. This boundary minimizes diffusion by centralizing availability logic while allowing Vehicle to own subsystem-specific redundancy hardware and Safety Services to own safety decision-making.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle subsystems implement proprietary redundancy or failover logic independent of NGHE Solution coordination, availability strategy may fragment. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry) that enforce NGHE Solution as the single source of truth for failover and diagnostics.

Risk: Predictive condition monitoring may require real-time processing latency that conflicts with NGHE Solution's higher-level orchestration role. Mitigation: Decompose DP7 into a lower-level predictive analytics module (owned by NGHE Solution but with dedicated compute/storage) that feeds availability decisions.

Risk: Automated diagnostics may need to interact directly with Vehicle hardware for deep inspection, creating tight coupling. Mitigation: Ensure diagnostics logic remains in NGHE Solution; Vehicle exposes diagnostic interfaces (e.g., register reads, sensor access) via Tasking/Telemetry contracts.

Risk: Teleoperation may need to override or manually trigger failover in edge cases, creating bidirectional control flow. Mitigation: Define explicit SafetyAlert and Tasking protocols that allow Teleoperation to request diagnostics or failover via NGHE Solution, not bypass it.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly addresses FR requirement for operational availability ≥0.97. The four pillars (redundancy + failover, hot-swap design, predictive monitoring, fault detection/isolation/diagnostics) are logically sound and collectively sufficient to minimize both failure frequency and MTTR. DP is measurable against acceptance criterion (availability ≥0.97 over evaluation period) and operationally coherent.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE: Predictive condition monitoring algorithms may require real-time latency and compute resources that conflict with NGHE Solution's orchestration role. Risk of splitting monitoring logic between NGHE Solution (decision logic) and Vehicle (sensor processing). Mitigation: Establish dedicated predictive analytics submodule within NGHE Solution with explicit compute/storage allocation and latency SLAs.

MODERATE: Automated diagnostics logic may need direct hardware access (register reads, sensor inspection) that creates tight coupling between NGHE Solution and Vehicle subsystem internals. Risk of diagnostic commands bypassing Tasking interface or creating undocumented hardware dependencies. Mitigation: Enforce all diagnostics interactions through formal Tasking/Telemetry contracts; Vehicle exposes diagnostic interfaces as first-class capabilities, not backdoors.

LOW: Teleoperation may request manual failover or diagnostics override in safety-critical edge cases, creating bidirectional control flow that could circumvent NGHE Solution orchestration. Risk of operator actions fragmenting availability strategy. Mitigation: Define explicit SafetyAlert/Tasking protocols that route all Teleoperation requests through NGHE Solution; document override conditions and approval workflows.

LOW: Vehicle subsystems may implement proprietary redundancy or failover logic independent of NGHE Solution, fragmenting availability strategy. Risk of subsystem-level decisions conflicting with system-level orchestration. Mitigation: Establish interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry) that enforce NGHE Solution as single source of truth for failover decisions; audit Vehicle subsystem designs for unauthorized redundancy logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Excellent architectural choice: DP7 is inherently a cross-cutting, system-level concern that must coordinate across Vehicle subsystems, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Centralizing availability orchestration in NGHE Solution minimizes fragmentation and preserves reusability. Vehicle (0.45 containment) correctly owns redundancy hardware and subsystem-specific failover mechanics but not the integrated strategy. Teleoperation (0.25) and Safety Services (0.30) correctly consume availability outputs without owning availability logic. Minor diffusion risks are manageable through interface discipline and submodule decomposition.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criterion (measured availability ≥0.97 over evaluation period) is directly testable via telemetry collection and availability metrics aggregation. NGHE Solution's Telemetry interface ingests subsystem health and performance data; SafetyAlert broadcasts availability status. Evaluation period and measurement methodology must be defined in worksite safety plan and operational policies. Recommend: (1) Define evaluation period duration and start/end conditions; (2) Specify telemetry sampling rate and availability calculation formula (e.g., uptime / total time); (3) Establish baseline and target thresholds for each redundancy pillar (MTBF, MTTR, prediction accuracy, diagnostic coverage).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: HIGH. DP7 is allocated to NGHE Solution with clear ownership of orchestration logic. Interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry (in) feeds monitoring/diagnostics; Tasking (out) executes failover/replacement; SafetyAlert (out) informs safety decisions; Network Link (inout) coordinates remote analytics. Responsibility boundaries are explicit: NGHE Solution owns availability strategy and decision logic; Vehicle owns redundancy hardware and subsystem-level execution; Teleoperation owns user control and override requests; Safety Services owns safety decision-making. Recommend: (1) Document Tasking protocol for failover commands (e.g., command format, latency SLA, acknowledgment); (2) Define Telemetry schema for subsystem health metrics (e.g., sensor readings, fault codes, degradation indicators); (3) Specify SafetyAlert content and broadcast frequency for critical faults.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Decompose DP7 into explicit submodules to manage diffusion risks. Recommend: (a) Redundancy Orchestration Module (owns failover decision logic and Tasking command generation); (b) Predictive Condition Monitoring Module (owns sensor fusion, degradation detection, and prediction algorithms with dedicated compute/storage); (c) Fault Detection & Isolation Module (owns diagnostic algorithms and fault tree analysis); (d) Availability Coordinator (owns cross-module orchestration and SafetyAlert generation). Each submodule should have explicit interfaces and latency/throughput SLAs.

REVISION 2: Formalize Tasking interface contract to prevent Vehicle subsystems from implementing independent failover logic. Specify: (a) Failover command format and semantics (e.g., 'switch_to_redundant_subsystem(subsystem_id, target_config)'); (b) Latency SLA for command execution (e.g., <100ms for critical subsystems); (c) Acknowledgment and status feedback protocol; (d) Audit mechanism to detect unauthorized subsystem-level failover attempts.

REVISION 3: Define Telemetry schema and sampling strategy to support predictive monitoring without creating real-time bottlenecks. Specify: (a) Sensor data types and sampling rates (e.g., 10Hz for motor current, 1Hz for temperature); (b) Subsystem health metric aggregation (e.g., rolling MTBF, degradation trend); (c) Buffering and transmission protocol (e.g., batch telemetry every 5s); (d) Latency tolerance for predictive analytics (e.g., 30s delay acceptable for trend detection).

REVISION 4: Establish SafetyAlert protocol and content specification to ensure Safety Services receives timely, actionable availability information. Specify: (a) Alert severity levels (e.g., CRITICAL for imminent failure, WARNING for degradation trend); (b) Minimum broadcast frequency for critical faults (e.g., <1s); (c) Alert content (e.g., fault code, affected subsystem, recommended action, confidence level); (d) Escalation rules (e.g., if CRITICAL alert not acknowledged within 5s, trigger emergency shutdown).

REVISION 5: Document Teleoperation override protocol to prevent circumvention of NGHE Solution orchestration. Specify: (a) Conditions under which manual failover is permitted (e.g., NGHE Solution unresponsive for >10s); (b) Request format and routing (e.g., Teleoperation sends 'request_failover' via SafetyAlert channel, NGHE Solution approves/denies); (c) Audit logging of all override requests and approvals; (d) Post-override diagnostics to identify why NGHE Solution did not trigger failover.

REVISION 6: Add explicit acceptance criteria for each DP7 pillar to enable granular verification: (a) Redundancy: 'All critical subsystems have N+1 or N+2 redundancy with <100ms failover latency'; (b) Hot-swap: 'Component replacement achievable in <30min without system shutdown'; (c) Predictive monitoring: 'Degradation detected ≥24h before failure with ≥90% accuracy'; (d) Fault detection/isolation: 'Root cause identified within <5min of fault occurrence with ≥95% diagnostic coverage'. Integrate these into worksite safety plan and operational policies.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR7 and DP7 are logically sound and well-allocated to NGHE Solution (0.92 containment). However, the DP_IM gate identifies MODERATE diffusion risks in predictive monitoring, automated diagnostics, and teleoperation override that require explicit architectural mitigation before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The recommended revisions (1-6) are not optional refinements; they are essential to operationalize DP7 and prevent fragmentation of availability orchestration across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Given high safety criticality, decomposing FR7 into sub-FRs without first resolving these architectural risks would propagate ambiguity into lower levels and create uncontrolled coupling between modules. Revise DP7 and IM7 to incorporate submodule structure, interface contracts, and SLA specifications before deriving sub-FRs.

revise.why: DP7 must be decomposed into four explicit submodules (Redundancy Orchestration, Predictive Condition Monitoring, Fault Detection & Isolation, Availability Coordinator) with formal interface contracts and latency/throughput SLAs. IM7 must specify: (1) Tasking protocol for failover commands with <100ms SLA for critical subsystems; (2) Telemetry schema with sampling rates (10Hz motor current, 1Hz temperature) and 30s latency tolerance for predictive analytics; (3) SafetyAlert protocol with severity levels, <1s broadcast for CRITICAL faults, and escalation rules; (4) Teleoperation override conditions and audit logging; (5) Acceptance criteria for each redundancy pillar (N+1/N+2 redundancy, <100ms failover, ≥24h prediction lead time, ≥95% diagnostic coverage). These revisions transform DP7 from a logical concept into an operationalizable architecture that prevents subsystem-level fragmentation and ensures NGHE Solution remains single source of truth for availability decisions.

assumptions: NGHE Solution has sufficient compute and storage capacity to host four submodules (Redundancy Orchestration, Predictive Condition Monitoring, Fault Detection & Isolation, Availability Coordinator) without exceeding latency budgets.
Vehicle subsystems expose health telemetry and diagnostic interfaces through standardized Tasking/Telemetry contracts; no proprietary backdoors or undocumented hardware dependencies exist.
Teleoperation system can route all failover/override requests through SafetyAlert channel and respect NGHE Solution approval workflow without introducing uncontrolled bidirectional control flow.

Safety Services can consume SafetyAlert broadcasts with <1s latency for CRITICAL faults and integrate availability status into safety decision logic without requiring direct access to NGHE Solution internals.
Evaluation period for availability ≥0.97 acceptance criterion is defined in worksite safety plan (duration, start/end conditions, measurement methodology, baseline thresholds).
open_risks: MODERATE: Predictive condition monitoring algorithms may require real-time compute resources that conflict with NGHE Solution's orchestration role. Mitigation: Establish dedicated predictive analytics submodule with explicit compute/storage allocation and 30s latency SLA; validate against NGHE Solution resource budget.

MODERATE: Automated diagnostics logic may create tight coupling between NGHE Solution and Vehicle subsystem internals if diagnostic commands bypass Tasking interface. Mitigation: Enforce all diagnostics interactions through formal Tasking/Telemetry contracts; Vehicle exposes diagnostic interfaces as first-class capabilities.

MODERATE: Teleoperation override requests may circumvent NGHE Solution orchestration in edge cases (e.g., operator manual failover). Mitigation: Define explicit SafetyAlert/Tasking protocols that route all Teleoperation requests through NGHE Solution; document override conditions and approval workflows; implement audit logging.

LOW: Vehicle subsystems may implement proprietary redundancy or failover logic independent of NGHE Solution, fragmenting availability strategy. Mitigation: Establish interface contracts that enforce NGHE Solution as single source of truth for failover decisions; audit Vehicle subsystem designs for unauthorized redundancy logic.

HIGH: Submodule interface contracts (Tasking failover command format, Telemetry schema, SafetyAlert protocol, Teleoperation override protocol) are not yet formally specified. Risk of implementation ambiguity and integration failures. Mitigation: Complete REVISION 2-5 before sub-FR decomposition.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Tasking protocol for failover commands: command format, semantics, latency SLA (<100ms for critical subsystems), acknowledgment/status feedback, audit mechanism.

Telemetry schema definition: sensor data types, sampling rates (10Hz motor current, 1Hz temperature), subsystem health metric aggregation, buffering/transmission protocol, latency tolerance (30s for predictive analytics).

SafetyAlert protocol specification: severity levels (CRITICAL, WARNING), minimum broadcast frequency (<1s for CRITICAL), alert content (fault code, affected subsystem, recommended action, confidence), escalation rules (e.g., emergency shutdown if CRITICAL not acknowledged within 5s).
Teleoperation override protocol: conditions for manual failover (e.g., NGHE Solution unresponsive >10s), request format/routing, audit logging, post-override diagnostics.

Acceptance criteria for each redundancy pillar: Redundancy (N+1/N+2 with <100ms failover), Hot-swap (<30min replacement), Predictive monitoring (≥24h lead time, ≥90% accuracy), Fault detection/isolation (<5min root cause, ≥95% coverage).
Resource budget validation: confirm NGHE Solution compute/storage capacity supports four submodules without exceeding latency budgets.

Vehicle subsystem audit: verify no proprietary redundancy/failover logic exists independent of NGHE Solution; confirm all health telemetry and diagnostic interfaces conform to Tasking/Telemetry contracts.
Worksite safety plan integration: define evaluation period duration, start/end conditions, measurement methodology, baseline thresholds for availability ≥0.97 acceptance criterion.
